title: Don't Fall For Texas Right to Life's Faux Abolitionism
author: James Silberman
orientation: Silberman dissects a Texas Right to Life legal-strategy document, arguing TRTL deceptively co-opts abolitionist language while actually proposing the same incrementalist sequence (PreNDA, Heartbeat Bill, Abolition Bill) it has always pursued. He contends the strategy is both rhetorically dishonest and legally incoherent, and that any incremental approach is doomed because it concedes the personhood of the preborn child.
audiences: Pro-life advocate considering whether TRTL's plan is genuinely abolitionist
Legislator weighing sequential versus immediate-abolition bills
Christian skeptical of incrementalism on theological grounds
Lawyer or law student evaluating the constitutional logic of partial bans

questions.question: Why does Silberman believe Texas Right to Life suddenly adopted abolitionist vocabulary in this document?
questions.audience: Pro-life advocate considering whether TRTL's plan is genuinely abolitionist
questions.answer: He argues TRTL was reacting to the surge in support for the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act and to abolitionism's growth in Texas, and he points out that searching TRTL's site shows no other articles using 'abolish' or 'abolition' in reference to ending abortion. The terminology shift is therefore a deliberate attempt to muddy the line between pro-life and abolitionist messaging.
questions.quote: Including the article title, the word “abolition” and its variants were used twelve times. A search for “abolish” and “abolition” turn up no other articles at their site which uses the a-word in reference to the eradicating of abortion from society. Texas Right to Life is not an abolitionist organization and their use of “abolition” in this article is a deceptive attempt to try to blur the distinctions between the pro-life movement and the abolitionist movement.

questions.question: How does Silberman characterize TRTL's claim that it has moved beyond incrementalism?
questions.answer: He treats it as a bait and switch: TRTL claims to abandon patient incrementalism for a 'bolder' approach but then immediately lays out a sequential, incrementalist legislative agenda. The supposed shift is rhetorical only and is meant to make incrementalism look like abolitionism to readers who can't tell the difference.
questions.quote: By using abolitionist language and then pretending to forsake incrementalism by embracing the same incrementalist strategy they’ve always pursued, there can be no question that TRTL is attempting to blur the distinctions between abolitionist and pro-life ideas in the minds of those who aren’t aware of the differences.

questions.question: What does Silberman say is internally contradictory about packaging PreNDA, the Heartbeat Bill, and an Abolition Bill into one carefully drafted bill?
questions.audience: Lawyer or law student evaluating the constitutional logic of partial bans
questions.answer: He argues PreNDA explicitly legalizes the killing of any baby who isn't being killed because of a genetic defect, the Heartbeat Bill explicitly legalizes the killing of a baby without a detectable heartbeat, and an abolition bill outlaws all abortion. Combining language that legalizes abortion with language that abolishes it produces an incoherent statute he calls 'schizophrenic nonsense.'
questions.quote: Any person with even basic understanding of how these laws work knows that the PreNDA bill and Heartbeat Bill directly contradict the abolition bill. The PreNDA bill explicitly codifies into law the legality of the murdering of babies for any reason other than the fact that the baby has a genetic defect. The Heartbeat Bill explicitly codifies into law the legality of the murder of a baby who doesn’t yet have a detectable heartbeat. A bill that has language to completely abolish abortion but also language that legalizes it is schizophrenic nonsense.

questions.question: How should a legislator respond to TRTL's insistence that abortion must be fought in a specific sequential order?
questions.audience: Legislator weighing sequential versus immediate-abolition bills
questions.answer: Silberman says the sequential ordering rests on no real legal or strategic reasoning beyond tradition. TRTL's only justification is that PreNDA must come first to force the Supreme Court to build a record affirming preborn humanity, but he sees no reason the Court would have to uphold lesser bills before it could uphold a bill of total abolition.
questions.quote: The second thing that needs addressing is the dogmatic assertion that abortion must be fought in this sequential order. There’s absolutely no legitimate reason to think this would be the case other than the fact that they’re claiming it. This assertion is a common one among pro-lifers, but there’s never any reason given other than “it’s just the way we’ve always done it.”

questions.question: How does Silberman use Justice Blackmun's footnote 54 in Roe to argue against incrementalism?
questions.audience: Lawyer or law student evaluating the constitutional logic of partial bans
questions.answer: He reads Blackmun as saying the Court will not tolerate a halfway position: if Texas truly believed the preborn human was a person, it would treat abortion exactly like any other murder, and treating it as an intermediate offense betrays that the legislature does not actually believe in personhood. Incremental bills therefore concede the abolitionist's foundational claim and invite the Court to strike them down for inconsistency.
questions.quote: Blackmun is explaining that if the State of Texas actually believed that the preborn human was a person, why didn’t they treat the murder of that preborn human like the murder of any other person. Texas treating abortion as an intermediate-level offense that isn’t actually murder betrayed the fact that Texas didn’t actually think the preborn child was a person.

questions.question: What posture does Silberman think states should take toward Roe and the Supreme Court?
questions.audience: Christian skeptical of incrementalism on theological grounds
questions.answer: He calls obedience to a court order permitting mass killing a moral sin and insists Roe should be ignored outright. Abortion should be abolished and the right to life upheld immediately, not gradually negotiated within a framework of submission to what he calls the Court's 'bloodthirsty tyranny.'
questions.quote: First, the court’s opinions must be ignored. It’s a moral sin to abide by an order from the Supreme Court or anyone else to allow mass murder. Roe must be immediately ignored, abortion must be immediately abolished, and the right to life must be immediately upheld.

questions.question: What action does Silberman want readers to take after reading the article?
questions.answer: He calls on readers to stop deferring to pro-life leadership, stop replicating the strategies that have failed for decades, demand immediate and total abolition, and actively rebuke any rhetorical move that smudges the line between pro-life incrementalism and abolitionism.
questions.quote: Stop taking the pro-life leaders’ word for it and stop fighting abortion the way it’s always been fought. Demand abortion’s immediate and total abolition and rebuke any deceptive effort to blur the distinctions between the pro-life and abolitionist movements.

related.title: Peeling Back the Texas Pro-Life Façade
related.reason: Companion critique of Texas pro-life institutions whose public posture conflicts with their legislative behavior.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Systematic version of the anti-incrementalism argument that Silberman applies here to TRTL's three-bill plan.

related.title: Tennessee Fetal Heartbeat Bill: A Step in the Wrong Direction
related.reason: Concrete case study of why heartbeat bills concede personhood, directly relevant to TRTL's heartbeat plank.

related.title: Abolitionist Perspective on the Texas Heartbeat Bill: Three Quick Observations
related.reason: Two years later, abolitionists evaluate the Texas heartbeat law TRTL had championed in this document.

related.title: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
related.reason: Compact framing of the strategic dichotomy that Silberman accuses TRTL of trying to obscure.

related.title: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
related.reason: Theological argument complementing Silberman's note that TRTL's appeal to 'divine vengeance' sits awkwardly with an incrementalist program.

related.title: The Holiness of God Destroys Pro-Life Incrementalism
related.reason: Develops the moral case that piecemeal bans are unfaithful, paralleling Silberman's claim that obeying Roe is a moral sin.
